title: Roast chicken
subtitle: Buttered, seasoned, roasted until golden
excerpt: A whole chicken roasted at moderate heat with butter, lemon, and garlic inside the cavity. The skin should be golden and crisp; the thigh meat should run clear at 75°C. Simple, reliable, and the most common Sunday roast in Britain. Serves four from a standard 1.5 kg bird.
sourceNotes: Roast chicken is the most common Sunday roast in the British home kitchen. The fundamental technique has not changed: butter under the skin keeps the breast moist, aromatics in the cavity perfume the meat from inside, and resting after the oven is what keeps the juices in when carving. The thermometer is the reliable check for doneness, colour alone is unreliable.

content.text: The key to a good roast chicken is moisture in the breast and crisp skin. Butter pushed under the skin keeps the breast meat from drying out during the cook. Starting the bird breast-side up for the full cook is correct, the fat in the back and thigh bastes the breast as it renders.

Internal temperature for chicken: 75°C at the thickest part of the thigh, away from the bone. The juices should run clear when you pierce the thigh. A 1.5 kg bird at 200°C takes approximately 1 hour 20 minutes, but ovens vary significantly, use the thermometer. Rest for 15 minutes under loose foil before carving.

Heat the oven to 200°C. Pat the {{chicken-whole}} dry inside and out with kitchen paper. Using your fingers, carefully loosen the skin over the breast and push half the {{unsalted-butter}} underneath, spreading it over both breast halves. Rub the remaining butter all over the outside of the bird. Season all over with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper}}. Push the {{lemon}}, {{garlic}}, and {{thyme}} into the cavity. Place in a roasting tin and roast for 1 hour 20 minutes, until the skin is deep golden and the thigh juices run clear when pierced. Check with a thermometer: 75°C in the thickest part of the thigh away from the bone. Transfer to a warm board, cover loosely with foil, and rest for 15 minutes. Carve and use the roasting tin juices for gravy.

items.fix: Always start with a room-temperature bird. If the breast is consistently overcooking, cover loosely with foil after the first 45 minutes and remove for the last 15 to crisp the skin
items.cause: Oven temperature uneven, or bird not at room temperature before going in
items.symptom: Breast dry and overcooked while thighs are just done

items.fix: Pat completely dry before buttering. If salted the night before and left uncovered in the fridge, the skin will be noticeably drier
items.cause: Bird too wet going in, or oven temperature too low

items.fix: Insert the thermometer at the thickest point of the thigh, angled away from the bone. Bone contact gives a false high reading. If the juices are pink at 75°C, cook for a further 5 minutes and check again
items.cause: Thermometer not deep enough or not positioned in the thigh correctly

content.text: Roast chicken overtook beef as the most common British Sunday roast in the latter half of the 20th century as chicken prices fell and beef prices rose. It is now the default Sunday lunch for most British families, faster than beef or lamb, cheaper, and universally accepted at the table. The pan juices make the gravy; the carcass makes stock. Nothing is wasted.
